title: Transaction Classification technical design
content: # Transaction Classification technical design
## Outcome

CLASSIFY is a deep, local module inside the .NET 10 Native-AOT Tally executable. It turns a bounded public LEDGER projection into deterministic suggestions, explanations, owner-approved rule versions, and replay-safe category assignment or correction. It owns no financial facts and never opens LEDGER storage. Evaluation may persist CLASSIFY outcomes, but it is read-only with respect to LEDGER; only `classify.apply.run` may invoke public LEDGER category mutation operations.

The design follows the Tally shape already selected by LEDGER and INGEST: explicit typed operations from one registry, vertical slices, source-generated JSON, explicit DI, concrete raw-SQLite storage, stable result values for expected failures, metadata-only diagnostics, and no daemon or network. It adapts the reusable intent of CORE without importing the Actions HTTP, EF, tenancy, or Angular deployment model.
## Interface exploration and selected shape
Three shapes were tested before entity capture.

1. A minimal three-entry facade (`evaluate`, `manage`, `act`) reduced method count but forced callers and tests to understand hidden action discriminators, lifecycle state, and different retry rules. The apparent simplicity was shallow.

2. A caller-optimized operation set exposed each real workflow explicitly. It made schemas, mutability, limits, errors, and idempotency inspectable at the interface and matched the PRD's canonical capability vocabulary.

3. A ports-and-adapters shape introduced interfaces for storage, rules, corpus input, filesystem, clock, and LEDGER. Only the shared LEDGER operation executor has multiple real consumers; the other seams would exist only for tests.

The selected hybrid is option 2 with selective reuse of the existing public LEDGER executor seam. Every public operation maps to one typed vertical slice. `LedgerContractClient` is concrete and resolves released LEDGER descriptors through the same executor used by the CLI. Raw SQLite, corpus reading, normalization, hashing, mapping, filesystem permission checks, and `TimeProvider` remain concrete until a second production implementation exists.

Deleting CLASSIFY would force the AI Agent Host or each future channel to own rule versions, deterministic normalization, conflict semantics, evidence retention, staleness, private validation, apply preflight, cross-store replay, feedback, and cleanup. The module therefore earns its boundary rather than forwarding calls.
## Code zones
One process contains seven explicit zones:
1. `Cli` maps token paths and structured input to a shared `OperationDescriptor` and writes one result envelope.
2. `Contracts/Classify` contains source-generated request, result, error, enum, descriptor, and compatibility types for the twelve stable operations.
3. `Features/Classify` contains one vertical slice per operation family: Contract, Evaluation, Rules, Apply, Feedback, and Recovery.
4. `Domain/Classify` contains closed rule vocabulary, normalization, matching, conflict, fingerprint, staleness, and lifecycle policies as pure deterministic code.
5. `Integration/Ledger` contains the concrete public-contract client shared with INGEST and CLASSIFY.
6. `Infrastructure/Classify` contains raw-SQLite state, migrations, private corpus reading, permissions, locks, and recognized temporary cleanup.

7. `Bootstrap/Features/ClassifyExtensions.cs` explicitly registers descriptors, handlers, concrete mappers, state services, and the Ledger client. Reflection scanning, a mediator, event bus, generic repository, provider-neutral unit of work, and plugin loading are forbidden.

Every asynchronous boundary accepts and passes `CancellationToken`. Public handlers return stable result values for expected failures; unexpected exceptions are sanitized once at the process boundary.
## Public operation contract
The shared Tally registry exposes exactly these first-release operation IDs and CLI paths:
| Stable operation | CLI path | Mutability and replay |
| `classify.evaluate` | `tally classify evaluate` | Persists one evaluation and outcomes; never mutates LEDGER; caller idempotency required. |
| `classify.outcome.get` | `tally classify outcome get` | Read-only. |
| `classify.apply.preview` | `tally classify apply preview` | Persists an expiry-bound authorization snapshot; never mutates LEDGER; caller idempotency required. |
| `classify.apply.run` | `tally classify apply run` | May invoke LEDGER assignment/correction; caller and per-item replay identities required. |
| `classify.rule.save` | `tally classify rule save` | Appends an immutable draft version; caller idempotency required. |
| `classify.rule.validate` | `tally classify rule validate` | Persists aggregate validation evidence; caller idempotency required. |
| `classify.rule.activate` | `tally classify rule activate` | Atomically creates and activates a new immutable rule-set version; caller idempotency required. |
| `classify.rule.retire` | `tally classify rule retire` | Atomically creates a successor rule set without the retired rule; caller idempotency required. |
| `classify.feedback.record` | `tally classify feedback record` | Appends feedback and at most one bounded proposal; caller idempotency required. |
| `classify.status` | `tally classify status` | Read-only safe lifecycle/status projection. |
| `classify.abandon` | `tally classify abandon` | Marks unreferenced work non-activatable and removes allowed derived payload; caller idempotency required. |
| `classify.cleanup` | `tally classify cleanup` | Applies the fixed retention and recognized-temporary policy; caller idempotency required. |

There is no generic invoke, run, manage, save, update, list, delete, HTTP endpoint, or hidden alias. The shared system schema operations discover the descriptors without opening CLASSIFY, corpus, or LEDGER data.

Complex input uses the Tally convention `--input -` or `--input @file`; sensitive payload and corpus paths exist only inside structured input, never as process arguments. Source-generated JSON rejects unknown fields. Every invocation writes exactly one versioned result or module-scoped error envelope to stdout and uses the shared Tally exit categories. Stderr contains correlation reference, operation ID, duration, and safe counts only.
## Public operation handlers and mapping
The vertical slices name concrete commands and queries rather than collapsing work into "implement backend":
- `EvaluateClassificationCommand`, `GetClassificationOutcomeQuery`, `PreviewClassificationApplyCommand`, and `RunClassificationApplyCommand`.
- `SaveClassificationRuleCommand`, `ValidateClassificationRuleCommand`, `ActivateClassificationRuleCommand`, and `RetireClassificationRuleCommand`.
- `RecordClassificationFeedbackCommand`, `GetClassificationStatusQuery`, `AbandonClassificationStateCommand`, and `CleanupClassificationStateCommand`.

`ClassifyContractMapper` is a pure boundary mapper between public DTOs and closed domain values. `ClassifyRowMapper` is a pure transformer between SQLite rows and domain records. Neither mapper opens storage, invokes LEDGER, chooses business outcomes, or activates state. Commands own validation, transactions, and lifecycle decisions. Queries own bounded projections and no durable mutation.

There is no EF Core or `DbContext`; the optional `DbContext` plus `IDbContextFactory` convention is explicitly inapplicable. `ClassifyStateStore` owns explicit SQLite connections and caller-owned transactions.
## LEDGER public projection and mutation seam

CLASSIFY uses the concrete shared `LedgerContractClient`; it never references `LedgerDb`, SQL, storage paths, feature handlers, or private domain types. The client resolves descriptor compatibility before any financial-data read.

Classification uses two closed purposes on the versioned `ledger.actuals.query` projection family, not a private query or direct table read. The next compatible LEDGER contract adds `itemProjection=classification_v1` and:

- `purpose=evaluation`, which fixes `categorizationState=requires_independent_decision` and excludes inactive transactions, already independently categorized transactions, active owned-transfer principal legs, and linked refunds or reversals whose category follows the original;

- `purpose=apply_preflight`, which requires a bounded `transactionIds` selector and returns every selected identity with Ledger-owned `assignable`, `correctable`, or `ineligible` mutation state, including already-categorized transactions needed for explicit correction;

- first-page `ClassificationProjectionDescriptor` containing projection version, store-generation fingerprint, snapshot ID and expiry, category-identity lifecycle fingerprint, and the active category catalogue as stable ID plus current display name;

- ordered `ClassificationProjectionItem` values containing ordinal, transaction ID, account ID, effective date, signed amount, source description, amount direction, mutation state, current category and allocation identities when present, transaction lifecycle revision, relationship revision, and allocation revision.

Display names are excluded from the category identity lifecycle fingerprint, so a same-ID rename refreshes output without staling an outcome. Archive, reactivation, or identity replacement changes the lifecycle fingerprint. Evaluation acquires the full projection before CLASSIFY persists an evaluation. Apply preflight requires every selected transaction identity to be returned or explicitly reported missing; it rejects the whole run if any item is ineligible or does not match the reviewed assignment or correction mode. Cursor expiry, missing ordinals, duplicates, version mismatch, or any page failure produces no evaluation or valid preflight.

Assignment and correction use compatible public `ledger.transaction.category.assign` and `ledger.transaction.category.correct` operations. Assignment asserts that no active allocation exists. Correction is always transaction-specific, carries an owner reason, and carries the exact expected active allocation identity. Both carry the transaction, relationship, and allocation revisions from apply preflight so LEDGER atomically rejects drift before mutation. Exact-rule broad apply can contain assignment items only. No CLASSIFY adapter weakens or translates LEDGER errors.

This purpose-scoped projection plus mutation-precondition extension is a hard upstream contract gate. Until its published-binary conformance test passes, the dependency remains assumed and implementation planning must keep the LEDGER contract work ahead of evaluation and apply work.
## Closed rule vocabulary and normalization

Rules are immutable typed data, not scripts or code. A rule version contains one or more conditions combined only with logical AND and proposes one active category ID. There is no OR, NOT, implicit priority, arbitrary expression, regular expression, wildcard, fuzzy match, executable plugin, or model score in v1.
The proposed `classification_v1` field and predicate registry is deliberately small:
| Field | Predicates |
| `description.normalized` | `equals`, `starts_with`, `contains_token_sequence` |
| `account.id` | `equals` |
| `amount.direction` | `equals` with closed `inflow` or `outflow` |
| `amount.absolute_minor` | `equals`, `between_inclusive` using exact Int64 minor units |

Normalization v1 applies Unicode NFKC, invariant case folding, punctuation-to-boundary conversion, and whitespace collapse while preserving digits and token order. It does not remove dates, amounts, reference numbers, stop words, or unknown tokens. Every normalized value is bounded before matching.

The registry is code-owned, versioned, descriptor-discoverable, and source-generated. `classify.rule.save` persists canonical typed condition rows and the selected normalization version. Unknown fields, predicates, values, duplicate conditions, empty rules, or archived/missing target categories fail before an activatable version exists.

The private projection/normalization spike remains a planning gate for this exact v1 registry. It must prove the fields and transforms against the owner-only fixtures without exporting payload. If the registry cannot satisfy the monitored recurrence and zero-incorrect-apply criteria, the plan must route back to the do-less workflow or revise this design before implementation; executors may not silently broaden the grammar.
## Deterministic evaluation and evidence

Evaluation first resolves the active immutable rule set and normalization version, then acquires the entire bounded LEDGER projection in memory. The engine processes projection ordinals in order and rule versions by stable rule-version ID. It returns exactly one outcome per item:
- no matching active rule: `no_suggestion`;
- one match, or multiple matches that all propose the same active category: one `suggestion` naming every contributing rule version;
- matches proposing different category IDs: `conflict` with no selected category;
- an input or fingerprint that becomes invalid before durability: `stale`.

No rule ordering selects a winner. Canonical SHA-256 fingerprints cover contract version, projection version, store generation, snapshot and expiry, category identity lifecycle, normalization version, rule-set version, and ordered item lifecycle revisions. Persisted `MatchEvidence` records rule version IDs, condition IDs, field keys, predicate kinds, and normalized-value hashes, never a reconstructed current payload. That is sufficient to identify what matched without retaining unrelated transaction fields.

One SQLite transaction persists the completed evaluation, all ordered outcomes, evidence, and aggregate counts. A failure before commit leaves no evaluation ID. Repeating an identical request and caller idempotency identity returns the same stored result; a different request with that identity returns a stable conflict.

`classify.outcome.get` reads only retained CLASSIFY state plus current category display names through the public LEDGER contract. It never reconstructs evidence from current transaction data. Unknown outcome and unavailable retained evidence remain distinct statuses. Staleness is computed by comparing the stored fingerprint dimensions with current public LEDGER and CLASSIFY versions; the response names each changed dimension and only permits re-evaluation.
## Rule lifecycle and private validation

`classify.rule.save` creates a new immutable draft rule version. Activation never updates that row. A validation run binds the candidate rule versions or candidate rule set, corpus fingerprint, expected-outcome fingerprint, projection contract, category lifecycle fingerprint, and normalization version.

The private corpus reader accepts one bounded owner-only JSONL source named inside structured input. It opens read-only without following a final symbolic link, verifies supported ownership and permissions, fingerprints the exact bytes before use, and streams rows through the same normalization and evaluation engine. Raw rows, descriptions, normalized values, amounts, expected outcomes, paths, and copies are never persisted. The durable report stores counts and fingerprints only: total rows, suggestions, no-suggestions, conflicts, stale rows, drift canaries, incorrect-application canaries, and unexplained conflicts.

Activation requires the exact current validation fingerprint, zero incorrect-application canaries, and zero unexplained incompatible-category conflicts. It creates a new immutable `RuleSetVersion`, membership rows, attribution, and an atomic active pointer in one transaction. Owner-approved broad apply is a property of that exact activated rule version and validation evidence; it is false by default. Retire or replacement creates a successor rule set and preserves the prior set and rule versions. A category rename resolves the current name; archive/reactivation or identity change invalidates activation evidence and affected outcomes.
If the corpus is unavailable, the last active rule set remains evaluable. New activation, normalization activation, and broad-apply authority fail closed.
## Apply preview and replay-safe saga

`classify.apply.preview` accepts either an explicit set of current suggestion outcome IDs, one exact broad-authorized rule version, or explicit correction items. It rejects no-suggestion, conflict, stale, expired, mixed-fingerprint, or unauthorized items. Correction items are never selected by broad mode and each carries its own reason.

The preview invokes `purpose=apply_preflight` for the exact selected transaction IDs. It requires every identity to be returned with current active category state, current allocation identity when present, lifecycle revisions, and an `assignable` or explicitly `correctable` Ledger-owned mutation state. It verifies the reviewed assignment or correction mode and active target category, then persists an expiry-bound `ApplyPreview` containing evaluation fingerprint, preflight snapshot and revisions, selection hash, item identities, modes, target categories, expected active allocations, rule versions, exclusions, counts, and owner attribution. Preview returns evidence but performs no LEDGER mutation.

`classify.apply.run` references one current preview and acquires an owner-only per-run OS lock. Before the first mutation it repeats `purpose=apply_preflight` for the entire selected set and revalidates every transaction, category, rule authority, projection dimension, mutation state, expected active allocation, lifecycle revision, and correction reason. Any missing, stale, ineligible, or incompatible item rejects the whole run before a LEDGER call.
For each item in deterministic transaction-ID order, CLASSIFY:

1. durably records the exact planned LEDGER operation, canonical request fingerprint, projection revision preconditions, expected active allocation identity, and a per-item idempotency key derived from the CLASSIFY apply identity and transaction ID;

2. invokes the compatible public LEDGER executor outside the CLASSIFY SQLite transaction; LEDGER atomically asserts assignment absence or the exact correction allocation plus all supplied revisions before writing;
3. durably records `applied`, `already_applied`, `rejected`, `failed`, or `unresolved` before advancing.

Process loss releases the OS lock. Resume replays only `planned` or `unresolved` items with the exact frozen request and key; LEDGER idempotency reconciles the commit-before-result and result-before-CLASSIFY-durability windows. Terminal item results never call LEDGER again. Reuse of an apply identity with a different preview, selection, category, mode, fingerprint, expected allocation, revision, or reason returns a stable conflict.
There is intentionally no distributed transaction across `classify.db` and the LEDGER store. The durable saga plus Ledger-enforced preconditions makes that gap explicit and testable.
## Feedback, status, retention, and cleanup

Feedback is append-only and references the exact outcome, transaction, evaluation, normalization, rule set, contributing rules, owner, reason, and trusted timestamp. A completed correction also references authoritative prior and resulting LEDGER allocation IDs. A deterministic proposal builder may create no proposal or one smallest-scope retire, narrow, or replacement proposal. It never creates an active rule, changes broad authority, or reconstructs missing historical evidence.

`classify.status` returns bounded safe metadata for rule, validation, evaluation, preview, apply, feedback, abandonment, and cleanup subjects. It does not reread private corpus rows or heuristically search payloads. Each nonterminal or failed state names whether CLASSIFY or LEDGER mutation may have occurred and one safe next operation.

`classify.abandon` applies only to unreferenced drafts, validations, evaluations, and previews. It appends a tombstone, makes the subject non-activatable, and deletes removable evidence-derived payload in the same transaction. Active rules, rule sets, apply provenance, feedback, or any state referenced by a LEDGER allocation cannot be hard removed.

`classify.cleanup` applies a fixed versioned policy; it accepts no arbitrary path. It removes recognized unlocked temporary artifacts, expired unreferenced previews, and already-abandoned removable payload, then records safe counts and identifiers. Startup performs the same ownership and recognized-name checks before accepting a new mutation. Symbolic links, unknown files, files outside the CLASSIFY root, and referenced history are never followed or removed.
## Persistence model

CLASSIFY owns one separate owner-only `classify.db` below the Tally data root. It uses Microsoft.Data.Sqlite, snake_case identifiers, foreign keys, WAL, `synchronous=FULL`, bounded busy handling, `PRAGMA user_version` migrations, explicit transactions, and a single supported owner writer. Directories are 0700 and database, WAL, SHM, lock, temporary, and report artifacts are 0600 where supported. Unsupported ownership or permissions block persistence.
The schema contains:
- store metadata, active normalization and rule-set pointers, and operation idempotency results;
- immutable rules, rule versions, typed condition rows, rule sets, memberships, and lifecycle events;
- validation runs and aggregate reports;
- evaluation runs, ordered outcomes, and bounded match evidence;
- apply previews, apply runs, planned item requests, terminal item results, and resume frontier;
- feedback records, bounded proposals, abandonment tombstones, and cleanup events.

Immutable version, outcome, apply-result, feedback, and lifecycle tables reject in-place UPDATE and DELETE through schema constraints or triggers. Mutable rows are restricted to explicit active pointers and nonterminal run-state transitions with guarded expected-state updates. Raw corpus rows and full LEDGER projection payloads never enter the database.

Flat JSON files were rejected because multi-row activation, outcome accounting, idempotency, saga recovery, and retained status become fragile. Sharing the LEDGER database was rejected because it destroys the public boundary and couples retention. A hosted database was rejected by the self-contained constraint.
## Feature areas
- Contract and Vocabulary: descriptors, source-generated contracts, stable errors, compatibility, limits, rule field/predicate registry, and normalization versions.
- Rules and Validation: immutable rule lifecycle, rule sets, private corpus validation, activation, retirement, and broad-authority evidence.
- Evaluation and Explanation: LEDGER snapshot acquisition, deterministic matching, conflicts, fingerprints, outcomes, evidence, explanation, and staleness.
- Apply and Feedback: preview authorization, all-item preflight, replay-safe LEDGER saga, correction feedback, and bounded proposals.
- State and Recovery: raw SQLite, idempotency, status, retention, abandonment, locks, startup recovery, and cleanup.
## CORE and platform compliance matrix
| Design axis | CLASSIFY obligation or explicit non-applicability |
| Save/upsert | Only `classify.rule.save`, and it appends an immutable draft version. Generic upsert is forbidden. |
| Get detail | `classify.outcome.get` and subject-specific `classify.status` return stable bounded detail. |
| GetGridList | No grid or UI. `classify.evaluate` returns an ordered bounded outcome set; status projections are not grid contracts. |
| GetLookupList | Active category identity and display data come from the snapshot-bound public LEDGER projection; CLASSIFY exposes no UI lookup DTO. |
| Delete/bulk delete | No public hard delete. Lifecycle retirement, abandon, and fixed-policy cleanup preserve referenced history. |
| Domain command endpoints | The twelve typed CLI operations are the domain surface; no FastEndpoints or HTTP route is created. |
| DTOs | Source-generated versioned request/result/error types live in `Contracts/Classify`; unknown fields fail closed. |
| Frontend key models | None. The recorded CHANNELS boundary owns future frontend contracts. |

| Commands and queries | The twelve concrete handlers named above are explicit vertical slices; evaluation and preview are commands because they persist CLASSIFY state while remaining read-only to LEDGER. |
| DTOMapper and EntityMapper | Pure concrete `ClassifyContractMapper` and `ClassifyRowMapper`; no storage access or business orchestration in mappers. |
| DbContext factory and optional shared context | Not applicable because raw SQLite is deliberate; `ClassifyStateStore` owns explicit connections and caller-owned transactions. |
| Expected paths | Greenfield paths are recorded on decisions. Missing-path warnings are explicit plan gates whose first tasks create the named slices and tests. |
| Permissions, roles, and auth seeds | Not applicable under the sole-owner OS trust boundary. No application users, permissions, roles, tenants, or seed templates. |
| Nav, menus, translations, frontend fixtures | Not applicable. CHANNELS owns navigation, translations, accessibility, and human conversation. |
| Business/persona seeds | No rules, categories, corpus rows, or owner records are seeded. P1 is the invoking OS owner; acceptance tests use private or generated fixtures only. |

| UI ownership | The recorded CLASSIFY non-goal and constraint defer every UI to the future CHANNELS PRD and design. CLASSIFY has no mockups, routes, components, frontend services, or browser tests. |

The applicable CORE obligations are: closed enums for system-owned states, CommandResult-style expected outcomes adapted to process envelopes, explicit DI, vertical slices, sanitized unexpected errors, RESTRICT-by-default retained history, pure mapping, security redaction, and contract-level integration tests. FastEndpoints, HTTP endpoint vocabulary, EF configurations, `IDbContextFactory`, PostgreSQL, Aspire hosting, RLS, tenancy, Angular signals, seeds, menus, translation catalogues, frontend key models, and browser E2E are explicitly inapplicable to this local CLI module.
## Verification architecture

- Pure unit/property tests cover normalization golden vectors, canonical serialization and hashing, every field/predicate pair, AND semantics, compatible same-category matches, incompatible conflicts, stable ordering, and all fingerprint dimensions.

- SQLite integration tests use real temporary stores and exercise immutable triggers, atomic rule-set activation, idempotency, evaluation accounting, preview persistence, guarded transitions, abandonment, migrations, and cleanup. SQL behavior is not mocked.

- Public LEDGER contract tests validate the classification projection, active categories, cursor completeness, rename controls, lifecycle staleness, transaction-ID preflight, assign/correct mapping, and incompatible versions through the shared executor.

- Published-binary process tests inventory all twelve descriptors, reject unknown fields, validate stdin/file input, check one stdout envelope and stable exits, deny network access, and scan stderr/logs/crash output for payload canaries.

- Private-corpus tests use ignored owner-only fixtures and generated fixtures; raw paths, rows, values, descriptions, and expected outcomes must not appear in graph, tracked artifacts, diagnostics, or durable reports.

- Apply recovery tests inject termination before and after planned durability, LEDGER commit, LEDGER result, CLASSIFY terminal durability, and item boundaries. They assert no duplicate allocation and an exact unresolved frontier.

- Performance tests evaluate 10,000 eligible transactions against 500 active rules and validate 10,000 corpus rows within five seconds and 256 MiB on the supported release host, with exact-limit and one-over-limit no-mutation controls.

The existing planned PRD test cases remain the trace anchors. Planning may add narrower unit cases but must not replace the published-contract, property, persistence, security, recovery, and performance gates.
## Greenfield implementation obligations
The repository currently contains documentation and graph artifacts but no application source. The design therefore records truthful future paths and treats current path-check warnings as plan gates:
- `src/Tally/Contracts/Classify/**`
- `src/Tally/Features/Classify/**`
- `src/Tally/Domain/Classify/**`
- `src/Tally/Infrastructure/Classify/**`
- `src/Tally/Integration/Ledger/LedgerContractClient.cs`
- `src/Tally/Bootstrap/Features/ClassifyExtensions.cs`
- `tests/Tally.Tests/Classify/**`

The plan must create these paths before any dependent implementation task can close. It must also keep the LEDGER projection contract and private vocabulary/corpus spikes ahead of classifier implementation. Missing or invalidated evidence routes back to LEDGER or the do-less workflow rather than authorizing an executor to invent fields, weaken conflicts, or broaden rules.
